message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 24
Recent changes: Added Math.ceil() to final premium return value for rounding up

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

content.text: I'll start the Refactor Phase by reading the necessary files.

content.content:
1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted inline `{ type: string }` to exported `type Item` for function signature clarity
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `items: Item[]` reads better than `items: { type: string }[]`
6	- Naming established: `Item` (type alias for item objects), `calculateBasePremium` (function)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced if/else conditional with `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table (`Record<string, number>`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- declarative map makes type-to-premium mapping explicit and treats all item types symmetrically
11	- Naming established: `BASE_PREMIUM` (module-level constant mapping item type to base premium value)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: added `Readonly` wrapper to `BASE_PREMIUM` type annotation
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- communicates immutability of configuration constant and enables compile-time mutation prevention
16	- Naming established: --
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): attempted destructuring `items[0]` into named binding but reverted -- mass increased from 8 to 15 with only marginal clarity gain; code already at local optimum after 3 prior refinement cycles
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- Change: replaced `Readonly<Record<string, number>>` with `as const` assertion and derived `ItemType = keyof typeof BASE_PREMIUM` union type
23	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `Item.type` is now a precise union instead of `string`, enabling compile-time validation of item types
24	- Naming established: `ItemType` (union type: `"sword" | "amulet" | "staff" | "potion" | "rune"`)
25	
26	## After cycle 6
27	- Change: replaced imperative `for` loop + mutable `sum` with `items.reduce(...)` single expression
28	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- reduce expresses "fold to sum" directly and eliminates 2 assignments (mass 28 -> 18)
29	- Naming established: --
30	
31	## After cycle 7
32	- Change: extracted `isComponentBlock(items)` helper, introduced `BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_PREMIUM`, and `COMPONENT_TYPES` named constants
33	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- inline condition `items.length === 3 && items.every(...) && BASE_PREMIUM[...] === 25` was opaque; extracted function makes block-detection logic self-documenting; mass +5 justified by clarity gain
34	- Naming established: `isComponentBlock` (predicate), `BLOCK_SIZE` (3), `BLOCK_PREMIUM` (60), `COMPONENT_TYPES` (ReadonlySet of component item types eligible for block discount)
35	
36	## After cycle 8
37	- Change: restructured `isComponentBlock` with early-return guard clause and extracted `blockType` named binding from repeated `items[0].type`
38	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- guard clause separates precondition from core logic; `blockType` names the concept more clearly than indexed access; mass +1 justified by clarity
39	- Naming established: `blockType` (local binding in `isComponentBlock` for the type that defines the block)
40	
41	## After cycle 9
42	- Change: removed dead `isComponentBlock` function (no longer called after green phase replaced it with inline group-by-type block detection in `calculateBasePremium`)
43	- Rationale: Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- dead code adds cognitive load and 11 mass units for zero value
44	- Naming established: --
45	
46	## After cycle 10
47	- Change: merged cursed surcharge loop into item iteration loop; introduced `cursedSurcharge` named accumulator separate from `total`
48	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named separation of base premiums (`total`) vs item-specific modifiers (`cursedSurcharge`) clarifies composition; reduced loops from 3 to 2
49	- Naming established: `cursedSurcharge` (accumulator for item-level cursed modifier, kept separate from base premium total)
50	
51	## After cycle 11
52	- Change: extracted `itemSurcharge(item: Item): number` helper; collapsed `cursedSurcharge`/`enchantmentSurcharge` into single `totalSurcharge` accumulator in main function
53	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- separates per-item surcharge logic from grouping/block-discount logic; names the concept; simplifies main loop body from 3 accumulators + 2 conditionals to 1 accumulator + 1 call
54	- Naming established: `itemSurcharge` (function computing all item-level surcharges for one item), `totalSurcharge` (accumulator in main function)
55	
56	## After cycle 12
57	- Change: renamed `calculateBasePremium` to `calculatePremium`; extracted `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` as named constants
58	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- function now computes full premium with policy-wide modifiers, not just base; magic numbers replaced with self-documenting constants
59	- Naming established: `calculatePremium` (renamed from `calculateBasePremium`), `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (2), `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (0.2)
60	
61	## After cycle 13
62	- Change: extracted inline magic numbers in `itemSurcharge` to named constants `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)
63	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- consistent with existing pattern of named rate/threshold constants; mass +24 justified by clarity
64	- Naming established: `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)
65	
66	## After cycle 14
67	- Change: replaced mutable accumulator (`let surcharge = 0; surcharge += ...`) in `itemSurcharge` with immutable named bindings (`const cursed`, `const enchantment`) and direct return
68	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named bindings make surcharge composition explicit; eliminating 3 assignments reduced mass from 43 to 29
69	- Naming established: `cursed` (local const in `itemSurcharge` for curse surcharge amount), `enchantment` (local const for high-enchantment surcharge amount)
70	
71	## After cycle 15
72	- Change: extracted inline `{ yearsWithMHPCO: number }` and `{ isFollowUp?: boolean }` to exported `Customer` and `QuoteOptions` type aliases
73	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- named types make `calculatePremium` signature self-documenting; consistent with `Item` type extraction from cycle 1
74	- Naming established: `Customer` (type for customer parameter), `QuoteOptions` (type for quote options parameter)
75

content.text:
All 24 tests pass. Now let me proceed with the refactor phase systematically.

## Step 0: Architecture Memory Loaded

I have read `architecture-notes.md` with 15 prior cycles of context.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

**Naming Evaluation**:
- Current name: `calculatePremium`
- Function purpose: Computes the insurance premium for a set of items, applying base premiums, block discounts, item-specific surcharges (cursed, high-enchantment), policy-wide modifiers (loyalty discount, first insurance surcharge, follow-up discount), a processing fee, and rounding up.
- Question: Does "calculatePremium" clearly reveal this intent?
- Assessment: Yes. The function calculates a premium. As more modifier layers were added, the name has stayed accurate -- the output is still a premium amount.
- Decision: Keep current name because it accurately describes what the function does.

Other naming considerations:
- `itemSurcharge` -- accurately describes its role (computes per-item surcharges). Keep.
- All constants are clearly named with domain-specific meaning. Keep.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Let me calculate the mass of the current implementation.

**Current Code Mass for `calculatePremium`:**

```
function calculatePremium(items: Item[], customer?: Customer, options?: QuoteOptions): number {
  const groups = new Map<ItemType, number>();       // binding(groups) + invocation(new Map) = 1 + 2 = 3
  let totalSurcharge = 0;                           // binding(totalSurcharge) + constant(0) + assignment = 1 + 1 + 6 = 8
  for (const item of items) {                       // loop + binding(item) + binding(items) = 5 + 1 + 1 = 7
    groups.set(item.type, (groups.get(item.type) ?? 0) + 1);  // invocation(set) + invocation(get) + binding(item.type x2) + constant(0) + constant(1) + assignment = 2+2+1+1+1+1+6 = 14
    totalSurcharge += itemSurcharge(item);           // assignment + invocation(itemSurcharge) + binding(item) = 6+2+1 = 9
  }

  let total = 0;                                    // binding(total) + constant(0) + assignment = 1+1+6 = 8
  for (const [type, count] of groups) {             // loop + binding(type) + binding(count) + binding(groups) = 5+1+1+1 = 8
    if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === BLOCK_SIZE) {  // conditional + invocation(has) + binding(type) + binding(count) + constant(BLOCK_SIZE) = 4+2+1+1+1 = 9
      total += BLOCK_PREMIUM;                        // assignment + constant(BLOCK_PREMIUM) = 6+1 = 7
    } else {                                        
      total += count * BASE_PREMIUM[type];           // assignment + binding(count) + constant(BASE_PREMIUM) + binding(type) = 6+1+1+1 = 9
    }
  }
  let policyPremium = total + totalSurcharge;       // binding(policyPremium) + binding(total) + binding(totalSurcharge) + assignment = 1+1+1+6 = 9
  if (customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD) {  // conditional + binding(customer x2) + constant(LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD) = 4+1+1+1 = 7
    policyPremium *= 1 - LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE;     // assignment + constant(1) + constant(LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE) = 6+1+1 = 8
  }
  policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE;  // assignment + binding(policyPremium) + constant(FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE) = 6+1+1 = 8
  if (options?.isFollowUp) {                        // conditional + binding(options) = 4+1 = 5
    policyPremium *= 1 - FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE;   // assignment + constant(1) + constant(FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE) = 6+1+1 = 8
  }
  return Math.ceil(policyPremium + PROCESSING_FEE); // invocation(Math.ceil) + binding(policyPremium) + constant(PROCESSING_FEE) = 2+1+1 = 4
}
```

`calculatePremium` mass: 3+8+7+14+9+8+8+9+7+9+9+7+8+8+5+8+4 = **131**

**Current Code Mass for `itemSurcharge`:**

```
function itemSurcharge(item: Item): number {
  const base = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];              // binding(base) + constant(BASE_PREMIUM) + binding(item) = 1+1+1 = 3
  const cursed = item.cursed ? base * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0;  // binding(cursed) + conditional + binding(item) + binding(base) + constant(CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE) + constant(0) = 1+4+1+1+1+1 = 9
  const enchantment =
    item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
      ? base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE
      : 0;                                           // binding(enchantment) + conditional + binding(item.enchantment x2) + constant(undefined) + constant(HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD) + binding(base) + constant(HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE) + constant(0) = 1+4+1+1+1+1+1+1+1 = 12
  return cursed + enchantment;                       // binding(cursed) + binding(enchantment) = 1+1 = 2
}
```

`itemSurcharge` mass: 3+9+12+2 = **26**

**Total Implementation Mass: 131 + 26 = 157**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Evaluate Rule 1: Tests Pass
All 24 tests pass. Confirmed.

### Evaluate Rule 2: Reveals Intent
Looking at the code, I notice the `calculatePremium` function has grown into a long function with distinct phases:
1. Group items and compute surcharges
2. Compute base premiums from groups (with block discounts)
3. Apply policy-wide modifiers in sequence
4. Return with rounding and fee

The function body is getting long (25 lines of logic). However, the phases are fairly linear and each section is short. Let me look for specific clarity improvements.

One thing I notice: the variable name `total` on line 46 is somewhat generic. It accumulates the base premiums from the group iteration. A name like `basePremium` or `groupBasePremium` would be more descriptive, especially since we also have `totalSurcharge` and `policyPremium` as separate concepts.

Another observation: line 54 creates `policyPremium` from `total + totalSurcharge`, and then the subsequent lines mutate `policyPremium` through multiple assignments. The name `total` is particularly confusing because it's not actually the total -- it's just the base premium portion before surcharges.

### Evaluate Rule 3: No Duplication
No obvious duplication.

### Evaluate Rule 4: Fewest Elements
The code is reasonably lean already.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll rename `total` to `basePremiumTotal` to better distinguish it from `totalSurcharge` and `policyPremium`. This improves Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) by making the three accumulator variables clearly name their distinct roles:
- `basePremiumTotal` -- sum of base premiums from item groups/blocks
- `totalSurcharge` -- sum of item-level surcharges
- `policyPremium` -- the combined premium after policy-wide modifiers

content.text:
All 24 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The rename changes `total` (a 5-character binding) to `basePremiumTotal` (a binding of the same cost). Mass is unchanged since we're only renaming -- same number of bindings, assignments, constants, etc.

**Mass Change: 157 --> 157 (delta 0)**

The mass is unchanged because this was a pure naming improvement (Rule 2), not a structural change.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Renamed `total` to `basePremiumTotal` in `calculatePremium` (better reveals intent)
- Mass: Unchanged at 157 (naming-only refactoring)
- Rule 2: Improved clarity -- the three accumulator variables now form a clear naming triad: `basePremiumTotal` (base premiums from groups/blocks), `totalSurcharge` (item-level surcharges), and `policyPremium` (combined premium after policy-wide modifiers). Previously, `total` was ambiguous and could be confused with the final total.

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Renamed `total` to `basePremiumTotal` for clarity (Rule 2)
- **Mass Change**: 157 --> 157 (no change, naming-only improvement)
- **Tests**: All 24 passing

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 16
- Change: renamed `total` to `basePremiumTotal` in `calculatePremium` to distinguish from `totalSurcharge` and `policyPremium`
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- generic `total` was ambiguous among three accumulators; `basePremiumTotal` clarifies it holds base premiums from group/block computation
- Naming established: `basePremiumTotal` (accumulator for base premiums from item groups, replacing former `total`)
